Last summer I spent three weeks hiking through the Scottish Highlands — just me, a 40-litre pack, and weather that changed its mind every hour. The first two days were glorious. Then the rain arrived and basically never left.

I met a sheep farmer named Callum near Kinlochleven who insisted I try his homemade blackberry wine. It tasted like cough syrup mixed with regret, but I drank two glasses because he was so proud of it.

The blisters on my right foot got infected around day nine. I fashioned a sort of bandage out of duct tape and a sock, which worked surprisingly well. A Dutch couple at the next bothy took one look at my feet and handed me actual first-aid supplies without a word.

By the second week I'd lost my phone charger, gained a deep appreciation for waterproof socks, and could identify three types of heather by smell. My journal entries got progressively shorter — from full paragraphs to single words like "wet" and "midges" and "chips."
